title: Evangelion Unit Airbone Transports
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion Unit Airbone Transports             Quote Post by BEsERk EVA01 » Mon Oct 15, 2007 4:34 am Another fine thread... 8) ANYWAY, you know how those Evas get transported and airlifted by those huge aircraft? Well, I've been thinking, and it's never been mentioned, not even in the FGD or whatever. How the hell can those transports airlift the Evas!?!? Even for their size, they've got to have even bigger engines! I imagine engines that can produce over 500, 000 lbs and they've got to be used for both the Evas and the aircraft. How do the transports airlift them!?!?! And how on Japan is there an airfield for those giant transports? The Evas themselves are big enough...Imagine a plane over 80% larger than those... :shock: :shock: :shock: :shock: :shock: :shock: :shock:

[]:             Quote Post by Fakuow » Mon Oct 15, 2007 5:45 am Given the rather elastic nature of Evangelions' size to suit plot purposes, I guess the Evangelions temporarily shrink to merely giant aircraft size.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Oct 15, 2007 7:58 am According to the classic height comparison: http://evamonkey.com/randomimages/evaheightcomparison.jpg ...the Eva units are about 140 feet to the top of the head. Doing some research on the internets, I found the AN-225: http://www.aerospaceweb.org/question/design/q0188.shtml http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antonov_An-225 With a length of 275 feet, and capable of lifting 550,000 pounds in addition to it's own weight, I would say that it is more than feasible for modern aircraft to lift an Evangelion Unit to a suitable deployment height. The Evas are pretty large robots, but they're not nearly as large as you might be thinking. I don't have a reference image for the transport plane handy, but I don't recall them being significantly larger than the Eva Units. Doing some further fuzzy research, the Blue Whale, believed to be the largest animal to ever live, was 110 feet in length, and up to 400,000 pounds. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue_Whale Though it may not be as lengthy as an Eva is heighty, it is an extremely bulky animal, and an Eva being as lean as it is could not possibly weigh more than a Blue Whale. And the An-225 being capable of lifting the largest animal to ever live leads me to believe that it is more than possible for a modern aircraft to lift an Evangelion, if designed for that purpose.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Oct 15, 2007 8:10 am The Eva Monkey wrote: According to the classic height comparison

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Oct 15, 2007 8:35 am I don't see what's so confusing. Just because the Evas have the strength of a living god does not mean that they proportionally way a LOT. They're heavy, but a large flying wing craft is capable of airing them. No problem.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 15, 2007 9:03 am V wrote: Just because the Evas have the strength of a living god does not mean that they proportionally way a LOT.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Oct 15, 2007 9:59 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: V wrote: Just because the Evas have the strength of a living god does not mean that they proportionally way a LOT.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Mon Oct 15, 2007 4:45 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: According to the classic height comparison: http://evamonkey.com/randomimages/evaheightcomparison.jpg ...the Eva units are about 140 feet to the top of the head.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Mon Oct 15, 2007 7:52 pm THE Hal E. Burton 9000 wrote: Heh, who knew the Soviets built their own version of the Spruce Goose and actually made it WORK? :o

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Mon Oct 15, 2007 8:55 pm BobBQ wrote: The Russians invented lots of cool things, but everyone west of the Urals have their heads too far up their asses to notice.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Mon Oct 15, 2007 9:04 pm THE Hal E. Burton 9000 wrote: Sure. There was the Tokarev, PPSh-41, SKS-45, Makarov, Kalashnikov, Dragunov, RPG-7, Abakan, Bizon, Saiga and the Mir Space Station.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Mon Oct 15, 2007 9:46 pm BobBQ wrote: The Russians invented lots of cool things, but everyone west of the Urals have their heads too far up their asses to notice.

[]:             Quote Post by BEsERk EVA01 » Tue Oct 16, 2007 3:30 am Anyway, how much do Evangelions even weigh as opposed to Antonov-225 capable of lifting over 40, 000 lbs of equipment, plus its own weight. However, I doubt that the An-225 can lift the Evangelion; sorry, but it just seems...well, strange. I can't seem to imagine the An-225 lifting such a gargantuan beast. Furthermore, the Eva would have to be taken away into separate pieces in order to decrease drag. However, the amount of space an Eva would take no matter how light it is, would over-fill the aircraft to its most-exceeding limits. I can imagine in 2015, mankind would've produced enough craft to lift an Eva with the necessary amount of lift and engine power to travel over LONG distances.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Oct 16, 2007 4:08 am BobBQ wrote: THE Hal E. Burton 9000 wrote: Sure. There was the Tokarev, PPSh-41, SKS-45, Makarov, Kalashnikov, Dragunov, RPG-7, Abakan, Bizon, Saiga and the Mir Space Station.

[]:             Quote Post by BEsERk EVA01 » Tue Oct 16, 2007 4:33 am Remember, only one fully operational Antonov 225 has been completed. The second one is currently under construction and the speed is decreasing due to finance issues. Most of you saw how they had to convert the remaining Sukhoi Su-37 Flanker-F to the less advanced Sukhoi Su-35 Flanker-E. If it wasn't for the finance, Russia would have much more technologically advanced warfare weapons. Anyway, one more question. How the heck could Japan hold over 9 Evangelion carriers on their already decreasing land???? And, how the hell would they land!!!??! Sure, they'd have reverse thrust, but even that wouldn't stop a gargantuan plane. They'd need kilometers long runways!

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Oct 16, 2007 4:58 am BEsERk EVA01 wrote: Remember, only one fully operational Antonov 225 has been completed. The second one is currently under construction and the speed is decreasing due to finance issues. Most of you saw how they had to convert the remaining Sukhoi Su-37 Flanker-F to the less advanced Sukhoi Su-35 Flanker-E. If it wasn't for the finance, Russia would have much more technologically advanced warfare weapons.

[]:             Quote Post by BEsERk EVA01 » Tue Oct 16, 2007 5:03 am Erm...yeah, sorry, I just got a bit pissed off at what happened today. Sorry man. I can be ignorant sometimes. But what would they use?

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Oct 16, 2007 5:16 am BEsERk EVA01 wrote: Erm...yeah, sorry, I just got a bit pissed off at what happened today. Sorry man. I can be ignorant sometimes. But what would they use?

[]:             Quote Post by BEsERk EVA01 » Tue Oct 16, 2007 7:00 am Perhaps they used some kind of thrust vectoring, except the power output must be enormous. Although when I saw the engines, they didn't look as if they could be thrust vectored. I also noticed they polluted a lot. :twisted:

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Tue Oct 16, 2007 7:37 am Alaska Slim wrote: Let's not forget it used LIQUID OXYGEN for fuel, which is HIGHLY EXPLOSIVE, it would make the shuttle a SERIOUS ground hazard, and you wouldn't be able to keep it fueled for very long, as it would corrode the tanks it was kept in.
